title: The Real Reason You Were Told Fat Is Bad

chunks.title: Historical Overview of Animal Fat Consumption
chunks.summary: The discussion starts by exploring the historical context of animal fat consumption, tracing changes from the early 1900s to the 1950s. It highlights how diet consisted mainly of animal fats like tallow and lard, with less emphasis on vegetables.
chunks.transcript: how did that become a thing Paul I'm sure you've gone over this a million times for your listeners but if you could sum it up how did we when did we flip that narrative where it was like hey these things are bad what happened in our world is it is it because we started having hamburgers and frying them in Greasy vegetables like what happened where we just went hey meets bad all of a sudden I think that um Nina Thai Schultz has done a really good job with this in her book The Big Fat Surprise I'm gonna have her back on the podcast soon but there's some history and it's probably right around 1950 after World War II because if you go back to the early 1900s I mean you know our great grandparents I mean your great grandparents might have been in Zimbabwe but my great-grandparents were New York you know you know in the United States or New Jersey and uh you know all the animal all the fat that we ate was from animals there were no seed oils before 1911 I mean cottonseed oil in the 1960s 1870s but Crisco 1911 was the first time we had seed oils but you know that's just one piece of the equation but early 1900s all the fat we ate was Tallow and lard probably from pigs that are raised way better than their raised today and butter that was all the animal fat that we got and we ate meat when we could get it like if you could afford meat in the early 1900s in the Great Depression World War One World two you ate that meat and people ate a lot less

chunks.title: Shift in Dietary Narratives
chunks.summary: The video explains the pivotal narrative change around the 1950s, particularly after President Eisenhower's heart attack. The association of saturated fats with health issues stemmed from selective studies, particularly the seven country study by Ansel Keys, which led to a demonization of animal fats.
chunks.transcript: just like inventions in the last few decades you know people ate potatoes I think you know but if you look at the menus from the early 1900s they're very different than we have today it's a few carbs quote unquote of like a potato and and meat but like that's where the saying meat and potatoes came from and then somewhere around the 1950s I think it was Eisenhower uh had a heart attack and there was a whole storm around like the narrative and you know like we're gonna blame this unsaturated fat and then Ansel Keys comes in and does the seven country studies which is totally um just looks at seven countries in the world where they could correlate saturated fat intake and heart disease and leaves out the other 16 studies uh there's another 16 countries where there was no association between saturated fat consumption and heart disease so it was like this epidemiology uh you know sleight of hand Voodoo and and then there was you know the American Heart Association received a huge Grant the equivalent of like 20 million plus dollars in 1951 or something from uh it was one of the food companies um it was Procter and Gamble so Procter and Gamble gave the American Heart Association I think it was 1 million or 1.5 million dollars in 1950 which the equivalent of 20 million dollars today and and then somehow you know like who knows you know the American Heart Association and Ansel keys and then the narrative completely

chunks.title: Influence of Food Industry
chunks.summary: The discussion addresses the influence of the food industry on dietary guidelines, particularly the American Heart Association's funding from food companies leading to a bias against saturated fats. It recounts how marketing transformed public perception of polyunsaturated fats as healthy alternatives.
chunks.transcript: is bad and that's butter and milk and animal fat and polyunsaturated fat is good where's that well it's Crisco and corn oil and you see these ads from the 1950s and 1960s you know mazzola your family you know like right polyunsaturate your family we can find some of those and put them in the YouTube video like there's a there's literally an ad it's got to be 1950s 1960s of a woman saying like tonight I polyunsaturated my family it's so freaking crazy dude so I think that that has been the narrative since the 1950s and it's all tied into seed oils and the saturated fat and then around that is like you know the vegetables too and I don't I mean vegetables are not the worst thing in the world but um it's been this sort of slow twisting of the narrative certainly away from saturated fat and then you're away from animals and then where do you have to go you go to Plants and then it's like oh well if you're not going to eat animals what are you going to eat the only thing left to eat is plants and then we get the food pyramid with uh six servings of grains per day or more I think it's you know and and like only a few like they I forget a six or twelve per day and you know some of those are whole grains but some of them are not even whole grains

chunks.title: Nutritional Misguidance and Food Pyramid
chunks.summary: The narrative explores the resultant dietary guidelines advocating for higher grain consumption and the idea that animal fats are unhealthy. This shift has led to widespread beliefs about the necessity of plants over animals in human diets.

chunks.title: Complexities of Cholesterol and Heart Health
chunks.summary: The video dives into the complexities of cholesterol types, especially LDL, and their relation to cardiovascular health. It discusses historical studies that highlighted how diets high in saturated fats did not correlate with increased heart disease rates, despite lower LDL levels.
chunks.transcript: with that sort of saturated fat versus polyunsaturated fat argument and then it gets really complicated when you start looking at the lipids because then the lipid hypothesis gets chunks.title: Conclusion and Current Dietary Perspectives
chunks.summary: The concluding thoughts stress the importance of re-evaluating dietary choices based on past insights. It suggests that current narratives surrounding animal fats may be misguided, inviting reconsideration on how they fit into a healthy diet.
